speaker: interviewer
text: And the children were sitting between the adults' legs. And do you remember any conversation in the cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember any conversation between me and anyone else. I can remember trying to look through the floor, a lot of cracks in the floor. Whenever the train stopped with a jolt, or otherwise, trying to listen to noises outside. And all I could hear was German, most of the time, or sometimes Dutch. But at no time did it indicate to me where we were. I recall people trying to get to the toilet. I remember the air quality, as we call it today, getting less beneficial. I remember, you know, my mother holding me, holding me near her, my father patting me on my head. But I don't remember any real conversations that I may have had either with my sister or with others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember using the bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not-- I do not remember to using the bathroom. No, I do not remember using the bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why do you think you were looking through the cracks of the bottom of the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I'm looking through the cracks because it was somewhat dark inside, although not pitch dark, as I recall. But I was looking really to the outside, to the outside, maybe to the outside world, maybe not to be in a confined place. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Maybe a bit of sunshine that I could detect through the splits in the wood, because those cars those days were all made out of wood. And most of them probably were fairly old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do always recall the door being slammed closed, with a sort of a final closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall when the train stopped? Did you know where you arrived, where you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the train stopping at various times during the trip. When it finally stopped, I didn't know where I was until we got out. When we got out, which then was the middle-- I later learned-- the middle of the camp Westerbork, there were lots of people walking around, all over the place. It was damp. Not really streets. It was more like hard and soft earth. Dark in color, because that part of Holland is very dark, the earth is very dark. And a lot of people, people with arm bands of different kinds. I don't recall seeing Germans. I do recall seeing what is named in Holland called the [FOREIGN LANGUAGE], which is blue uniforms. I would compare it maybe, here, I don't quite know, really. It's not really the police. It is more than the police. It is probably-- in Germany, you would call it [FOREIGN LANGUAGE]. It's not just the ordinary police. It's more than the police. It's like in Spain would call it the [FOREIGN LANGUAGE]. It's more than just police.

speaker: interviewer
text: They are the ones that guarded the camp. And they were what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dutch. They were all Dutch. They were not Germans. They were Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out where you are and what this place is all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: People told us, this is Westerbork in the province of Drenthe. And as I said earlier, from school I knew where Drenthe was. It's very near the Dutch border. And it's northeast Holland. There's only two other provinces north of that. So it's a place that I would never have gone, and never-- I knew about it, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think this place was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I knew what it was very soon. The reason I knew what it was very soon was that people were put on transport from there. People were-- they would come around the barrack with a list and call a person's name. And that person would be told that tomorrow morning at 6 o'clock, or 5 o'clock, or whatever time, they had to be in a certain part of the camp. And then, of course, while I was there I saw different trains leaving there. In the same place as we had arrived, I saw trains leaving, in those days steam trains, of course, pulled by locomotives leaving from Westerbork and all cattle cars, only cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never saw a normal train leave there. You know, children are usually excited about railroads and trains. Did you feel any such excitement?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I had, by already that age in my life, been a quite frequent traveler between Holland and England by ship, I've always been fascinated by travel and the world. I was less fascinated by travel after being taken from Amsterdam east in the train to Westerbork. I was never excited about those trains. I must have felt-- I must have felt at the time-- some sort of ominous feeling about those trains, because I had never, until that first time, done any of my traveling in a car that was meant for cattle. I'm sure I must have been, as I was then, nearly 11 years old. I must have had forebodings about that mode of travel, to put it in a nice way. I do remember, and it's been a terrible thing to me in my life, remembering that I saw one of my uncles being in the train, being in one of those cattle cars, and those cattle cars on the top, in certain parts of its four walls, have little grills. And I remember seeing my Uncle Max, who was an uncle of mine called Max Rabbie. He was a big man, because the Rabbie family are big. And I remember seeing his face and him waving his arms through the bars. That stayed with me terribly, terribly, all my life. Whenever I talk about any of those experiences like I'm doing now to you, Renee, all of it is in front of my eyes. Although I'm looking at you, I'm looking at that scene 50 years ago. Another thing I would like to tell you, people started to get lice in their hair. And I remember-- why I remember it all that time-- that people used to go in the quarantine. And they used to get their heads shaved so as not to infect the rest of the camp. And I remember as a child, we used to sing a song. And I remember that song to today, and I will sing it if you like. But it's Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the song goes something like this. [SINGING IN DUTCH] If you like, I'll translate it for you. It says, we are going to disappear. We are going to the quarantine. And we'll return with a bald head, because we will be shaved, because lice were on our heads. I remember that all these years, this song, quite incredible really that I should remember a song. But as a child, you see, it was like a kind of almost jocular, at that time, to a child. But its deeper implication wasn't apparent to me then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember children in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I remember children in Westerbork. We used to play with children, play. Play hide and seek running around the barracks. Playing with-- what do you call them in English. We called them, in Dutch, [DUTCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Marbles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marbles, yes. Playing marbles on the earth. When it rained, of course, we couldn't play because the earth became a terrible mud, terrible mud. At that time, and all through the time in the camp, for my outside clothes I had been given by Jopi's father, a pair of ski boots. Years ago, ski boots were made of leather, not other materials like today. And had thick woolen socks. And I had an overcoat which was given to me by my aunt in England before I returned to Holland. And the overcoat was a hand-me-down from a cousin of mine who was older than me. Didn't fit him anymore, so it was given to me. And I wore that overcoat. As a matter of fact, I wore that overcoat the whole war. It was a long, British properly made, woolen material overcoat. And I wore that, of course, from the time we were taken away from Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Westerbork about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What comes to your mind mostly when you think of Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trains. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Just the trains, the people being called to go on the trains. And we left, all the time staying there, because then I realized we were being held there because A, of my mother's place of birth being England, and because I then got to know that when we were taken from Holland to Westerbork, all the diamond people were taken on the same transport, which is known. It was known then and today is known as the diamond transport, diamond transport. It's name diamond transport. And everybody that was to do with the diamond business in Amsterdam, and indeed in Holland, all went on that same transport. And all finished up in Westerbork together. All diamond people. About six months. Then of course, it became the winter. And it's very cold in Holland in the winter, because Holland is very flat. And the northeasterly winds of the North Pole is very, very, very cold in the winter. But up to that time, we were well fed. Although food in Holland was not in abundance in the war, before we were taken away, we still had enough to eat. I was warmly dressed. The food was less abundant in Westerbork, but nutritionally we had not suffered up to that point that it was affecting me, as far as I can remember. So I could withstand the cold, personally. My sister was more affected by the weather, by the cold weather, by the lack of choice of food, because we were only given-- we only ate what we were given, because my sister had always been a very poor eater, suffered from asthma. Whereas I would eat anything. I was always a good eater. I was basically strong. My sister was much less so than I. She suffered already in Westerbork with the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live? How did you live in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a barrack with other people, together with my parents. There was one stove, I think you call it in English or in America a potbelly stove, with wood. They put the wood in. And everybody would fight to get near the fire, near the stove, because that was the warmest part of the barrack or in your bunk. Sleep dressed. Never go undressed to go to bed, to be in the bunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the stove was the warmest place. But of course, being small still, even at that age, we could always get nearer the stove than the others. So that's how we kept warm. But as I say, our warmth, I think, was a lot helped by our physical strength at that time to keep warm. But it's terribly cold, terribly cold in the winter in that part of Holland. Did you have covers on the bunks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all had like what one would call a gray army blanket. Yes, we'd been given one each, because we didn't bring any blankets with us, only our rucksack and our shoulder bag. But we had no bedding with us. We only had our clothes with us that we were able to take and that we were allowed to take. I think there were certain things allowed to be taken, and not allowed to be taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as most Dutch citizens those days were very law abiding, I think we all did what we were told. We, I think, left Westerbork in 2nd or 3rd of January, 1944. And we, believe it or not, were put in passenger cars, passenger rail cars, with the blinds drawn down. What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought at the time, it was strange. Other people had been taken in transports in the cattle cars. Why are we going in an ordinary train like I'd been used to as a child? And I personally thought we were going to go either to a neutral country, or they were going to take us to England. I never thought we were going to a bad place. I thought we were lucky to be in those trains and not in the others which I had seen. The train left, and we traveled in the night, in the dark. It stopped a few times. We could carefully open a little thing you pulled out, which were all pulled out. There were German SS on the train that walked through the cars. We were with the other diamond people, that my mother and father knew, that they had associated with and knew because they all knew each other, all Jewish, obviously. We just traveled. The train was going. We didn't know where it was going, in which direction. I could hear the steam whistle from the locomotive. We could see, sometimes, odd little light go by. And it would come to a halt at times. There'd be Germans walking outside and talking. They'd be on the train, as I said. And that's basically what I remember of the actual journey. We were all sitting next to each other. Nobody made me feel afraid, because they're all Dutch people that my parents knew, or knew of. And we were all, so to speak, in the same boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I recall that I had. I don't recall myself having conversation. I recall there was talking in the compartment between the adults. But not that I recall that I was ever talking to anybody. I may indeed have talked to others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you heard? The adults talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the noises of trains, of railway lines, of sleepers going by as the train goes, of points where the rails cross to go in another direction, all noises associated with trains of those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about conversation between the adults?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall it. I don't recall what they were saying, what they were talking about. Talking in low, low tones. But not actually the substance of what they were talking about. I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was this journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't quite know how long the journey was. I know it was overnight. It may-- there again, thinking about it now, it may have been one night, one overnight. It may have been, maybe two overnights, although I doubt that. Because of the physical distance involved, I don't think it, under normal circumstances, would have taken more than one overnight. Certainly, the whole day-- that's quite possible-- and one night. But I don't think more than two nights, I doubt very much. I doubt very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food on the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall being given any food. But I think my mother had food with us, bread and cheese, that she had with us from the camp still from Westerbork in one of those shoulder bags that we had. The day of the final stop of the train, if I could say that, the day of arrival, I remember that was very early in the morning. Nobody knew where it was. As it was in an area completely surrounded by pine trees. We were ordered out of the train onto a platform, but not a platform that's made like in a regular station, a platform more that in English is called a siding. A siding being really a place where a train gets put to be worked on or to be left or something like that. And the platform, as such, wasn't made out of proper materials. It was like more earth. I remember getting out of the train. And all-- there was a platform where the train was. And behind the train-- or shall I say on the other side of the train-- were German soldiers with leather coats, rifles slung over their shoulder on their back, and virtually each one had what you call a German shepherd dog. They were on the other side of the train. And on this side of the train, on the platform side, were a few obvious officers with black leather coats, SS, all SS. And we were told to form into a column, with five in a row, five people. And we had to carry our luggage, the stuff we had, which was the rucksack. I had no suitcases. I personally, my family had just a rucksack and the thing over our arm. And we were marched off the siding. And eventually what we later learned to be Bergen-Belsen, the camp. Known as Belsen, but its proper name is Bergen-Belsen, because the little village it's near is called Bergen, which is in a part of Germany, which is on the Luneburger Heide, in the Northwest part of Germany, between Hanover and Hamburg. It's a part of Niedersachsen. And the provincial capital there is Celle, C-E-L-L-E. But the camp was outside of Bergen. That's why it's called Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got to the camp, they opened the gates, which was barbed wire. And we stopped once we were inside. We stopped at a barrack. And like two by two the people went into the barrack. And outside the door of the barrack was a German SS. And we had to walk up two steps to go into the barrack. And everybody went into that barrack and came out the other side. Eventually, I had to go with my father into the barrack. And I remember, at that time, I think it was the first time I was physically touched, where the German soldier pushed his rifle in my side-- I mean, I was only like this big-- to put my hands up. And that way we stepped up the steps inside. Looking at it now, thinking about it, whether it was to show that we didn't have anything in our hands, I just don't know. Once we were inside, we didn't have to stand like that. And then they asked-- there was two Germans sitting down there in uniform, SS, with papers in front of them. Asked us some questions, which I don't remember now. And then we were made to go out the barrack. Then my sister and my mother came, and the other people. And then we were taken to a particular barrack. When we got to that barrack, I'll never forget how it struck me. It was a new building, a new wooden barrack. Half of it seemed to have tables and chairs, with a potbelly stove, with wood next to it. The whole barrack made out of wood. And the other half had bunks, bunk beds. The whole place smelled of wood. As I found out, the mattresses that were in the beds were full of wood shavings. And I remember that smell to today, that the whole place smelled, not obnoxiously, of wood. Once we got in there, the people got in there, they made the fire because it was cold, freezing cold in Germany in January. And we were in that barrack. I now know, many, many years later, which part of the camp that was, what the area of the camp was called. I know all that. But at the time, it was quite nice in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel when this Nazi soldier pushed you with his bayonet?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, afraid. At the time, afraid. Afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember afraid of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afraid of him. Afraid of him. Afraid of would he hurt me. I don't think I thought he'd shoot me. I was afraid he would hurt me, physically hurt me. Not shoot me, but physically hurt me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your father reacting?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I don't. I don't. Whether I've blocked that out of my mind, I don't know. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family stayed in this barrack together?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father, my sister, and myself. In another barrack was my aunt and her son, my cousin John, and my late uncle, Samuel Fransman, his name was. In answer to your question, my immediate family, my parents and I, and my sister, were in the same barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I recall at that time. Not that I recall at that time. Later in the camp, yes, but not at that particular time when we arrived there. Because that barrack was basically only for the diamond people. And the other people, who were in Belsen for other reasons, were in other barracks, although in the same part of the camp, in other barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will stop again for a minute.
